text: The surgeon inserts the first camera port through the fifth intercostal space at the anterior axillary line, followed by the second port through the second intercostal space at the midclavicular line. A third port is introduced, and the camera view is adjusted to a higher perspective. The video reveals the pericardium, diaphragm, and visible adhesions in the chest cavity.

The surgeon uses a hook ligature device to carefully dissect the right lung from the thymus, ensuring the phrenic nerve on the superior vena cava is protected. The ligasure technology is employed to seal small vessels like the thymic vein with minimal heat dissipation. The surgeon points out the phrenic nerve and proceeds to clear fatty tissue from the diaphragmatic recess. A sub-xiphoid port is utilized for a 12mm trocar with a 30-degree camera to inspect the opposite phrenic nerve and facilitate tumor removal. The surgeon then uses the hook to open the left pleura beyond the diaphragm, maintaining CO₂ insufflation at 8 to 10 mmHg.

The surgeon enters the left pleura and restores ventilation to both lungs using a double lumen tube, with CO₂ pushing both lungs down. The pericardium is opened early to remove a large disc with the thymic mass, ensuring complete resection. The thymus and thymic fat are stripped from below upwards, carefully navigating around the phrenic nerve using a ligature device. The pleural reflection is opened beyond the rhema vein, revealing the superior vena cava (SVC) and the innominate vein tracking medially.

The surgeon dissects medial to the rhema vein over the thymic mass using a hook, addressing small vessels from the lima and rhema with a ligasure. The thymus is stripped off the sternum, and the opposite pleura is opened widely. The pericardium is opened, revealing no invasion by the thymic mass. The surgeon continues dissection superiorly, focusing on the opposite phrenic nerve and superior horns, while carefully navigating potential vessels from the reamer and lemur veins and arteries.

The surgeon dissects over the innominate vein, anticipating the presence of a thymic vein extending into the thymus. Using a ligature device, the surgeon addresses vessels encountered during dissection, avoiding the use of clips. The thymic mass is large and adherent, prompting the surgeon to focus on easier sections first. A 12mm sub-xiphoid port is inserted below the xiphi sternum, allowing for camera access to visualize the left phrenic nerve. Adhesions to the lungs on both sides complicate the procedure. The surgeon attempts to manipulate the thymoma using instruments from the right side, while also exploring the area over the innominate vein and identifying the lemur and reamer veins and arteries. The dissection progresses towards the right superior horn, aiming to remove all tissue up to the thyro-thymic ligament, where a tumor is visibly extending further than anticipated.

The surgeon uses energy to divide the Rheema vein, improving the view of the right superior horn and thyrothymic ligament. The innominate vein is visible tracking underneath, and the innominate artery is exposed, indicating the correct dissection plane. The surgeon pulls down the right superior horn, ensuring no fatty tissue remains above, and uses ligature to address vessels in the thyrothymic ligament, achieving a dry surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to head up, reducing venous pressure and controlling bleeding. Using a Johan blunt tip dissector and ligature, the surgeon tracks across the innominate vein to locate the thymic vein, applying energy to dissect it. The left superior horn is pulled down using the ligature, with the lima vein visible in the distance. The surgeon creates a disc of pericardium using a sub-xiphoid camera and hook, addressing the opposite phrenic nerve. Due to poor visibility from the sub-xiphoid camera, a 12-millimeter tri-stapler is used to separate the lung from the thymic mass, improving the view of the phrenic nerve. A new port in the left chest provides a clearer view, allowing precise dissection near the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon uses a single five-millimeter port on the left side to maintain vision of the phrenic nerve while lifting the tumor and dividing it from the phrenic using a ligasure. The divided piece of lung is visible. The pericardium is safely divided, revealing the pulmonary artery and innominate vein. The tumor is retracted using its weight, allowing dissection off the pulmonary artery. Both phrenic nerves are kept under vision, with the tumor pulled back into the right chest. The innominate vein is visible in the distance, and the surgeon uses a hook to remove fatty tissue beneath the innominate vein and above the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a hook ligature for dissection, grasping, and cutting, demonstrating its multifaceted capabilities. Three instruments are visible in the chest for the final part of the procedure. The tumor is placed in a bag and removed through the sub-xiphoid port. The surgeon checks for bleeding before removing the tumor, noting the loss of CO₂ upon removal. A size twenty French drain is placed due to lung stapling, either removed immediately post-operatively or a few hours later.
